sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
A seizure is abnormal electrical activity in the brain causing transient changes in behaviour, movement, sensation, or awareness. These discharges are typically spontaneous, uncontrolled and transitory. Seizures are a sign of an underlying disorder.
- Idiopathic (self-originating and without known cause, most epileptics)
- Symptomatic (known medical or traumatic cause) Status epilepticus (SE) is defined as:
  - A seizure lasting >5 minutes, or
  - Recurrent seizures without return to baseline consciousness between episodes.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Motor: Tonic-clonic movements (stiffening/jerking), focal limb twitching, head nodding
- Non-motor: Staring, confusion, lip-smacking, sensory hallucinations, unresponsiveness
- Autonomic: Tachycardia, hypersalivation, apnoea, incontinence
- Post-ictal phase: Fatigue, confusion, headache, amnesia
- Prolonged seizures or SE can be associated with hypoxia, hypercarbia, lactic/respiratory acidosis, hyperthermia, hypertension, tachycardia, hypo/hyperglycaemia, hyperkalaemia

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Anticipate airway obstruction, aspiration risk, and post-ictal hypoventilation
- Midazolam may be given by approved routes in line with scope and local protocol
- Pharmacological management of non-convulsive or atypical seizure types requires consultation with the on-call doctor or RFDS medical support
- Remove hazards and protect the head; use restraint only to prevent immediate injury
- Generalised seizure activity is time critical when it lasts more than 5 minutes, recurs without recovery, or is associated with injury, pregnancy, infection, or persistent reduced consciousness
- Common causes include epilepsy, hypoglycaemia, CNS infection, head trauma, drug toxicity, stroke, and alcohol withdrawal

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Protect the patient from injury while the seizure is occurring
- If the seizure is prolonged or recurrent, consider midazolam via the approved route under local protocol: IM 5–10 mg; intranasal 5–10 mg (use higher end due to lower bioavailability); buccal 5–10 mg. Weight-based alternative: 0.1–0.2 mg/kg, maximum 10 mg
- Complete Primary Survey once it is safe to do so
- Maintain the airway as best as possible; never place fingers or objects in the mouth during active seizure activity
- Suction if required and place the patient laterally once safe, including left lateral positioning in pregnancy where appropriate
- Provide oxygen and assist ventilation if indicated
- Record seizure onset time, total duration, blood glucose, temperature, oxygen saturation, and conscious state after the event
- Identify and treat reversible causes where evident and maintain a low threshold for senior consultation

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Establish vascular access if the seizure is ongoing, recurrent, or another serious diagnosis is suspected
- Consider point-of-care testing where available and clinically useful
- Consider IV midazolam, titrated to effect, in line with protocol for persistent seizure activity
- Consider IV glucose if hypoglycaemia is present or strongly suspected
- Escalate rapidly for status epilepticus, airway compromise, trauma, pregnancy, fever, or failure to recover as expected

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Complications and side effects of seizures can include:
  - Incontinence
  - Secondary injury (including intra-oral trauma)
  - Aspiration
  - Hypoxia
  - Hyperthermia
  - Dysrhythmias
  - Brain damage
  - Death
  - ESO’s, MESOs and other similar levels should have a low threshold for calling the current poison permit holder or RFDS
